topic_title: If / else — writing decision logic in code

format_discipline.first_line: # If / else — writing decision logic in code
format_discipline.detail: No frontmatter. Correct 6-section structure in prescribed order. Sidecar reading.meta.json present and valid.

length_budget.detail: 1316 words at 230 wpm ≈ 5.7 min; round-up estimate 7 min. In range [6, 8] ✓.

citation_discipline.detail: All 5 primary resources cited at least once. Bibliography in References section complete.

forward_references.detail: No forward references detected. ValueError named-and-deferred to 11.6 in one sentence — acceptable. Spec-first named-and-deferred to 11.7 in one sentence — acceptable. All code uses only concepts from 11.1–11.5.

beginner_readability.detail: All terms defined on first use (comparison operator, if statement, elif, input()). Comparison operators presented in a table — concrete before abstract. No jargon dumps. Paragraphs ≤ 4 sentences throughout.

formatting.detail: Operator table in Key Concepts. Code blocks for each concept. Numbered list for 3 if-statement syntax rules. Line-by-line notes in Worked Example. No walls of text.

scope.detail: Content stays within if/else, comparison operators, elif, compound conditions, input(). No material taught beyond 11.5 headline.

corpus_faithfulness.detail: All claims map to corpus §§5.1–5.5 and §6. No new claims introduced by the reading.

verdict: PASS (cycle 1). 1316-word, 7-min reading is clean: no frontmatter, correct section structure, all 5 resources cited, no forward references, no scope-creep. Diagram embedded correctly in Key Concepts. Beginner-clear throughout.
